title: Cross-Attention Mechanics
concept: Cross-attention uses the decoder's query to match against the encoder's keys and retrieve values, bridging input and output domains.

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of Cross-Attention?

options.text: It ignores the input text entirely.
It connects the decoder to the encoder's information.
It only looks at the output text.

exercises.explain: Cross-attention connects two different sequences (like input and output) using queries, keys, and values.

cards.title: Bridging Two Worlds
cards.body: How does a translator AI connect the sentence you typed to the sentence it is writing? It uses a smart matchmaking system called cross-attention to look back at the original text at the perfect moment.

cards.title: What is Cross-Attention?
cards.body: Cross-attention is a mechanism where one sequence (like a translating decoder) looks at another sequence (like the original encoder input). It uses Queries from the target side to search through Keys and retrieve Values from the source side.

cards.title: The Deli Order Analogy
cards.body: Imagine you are at a deli counter. You hold a ticket showing what sandwich you want (the Query). The deli worker has a list of ingredients on the board (Keys) and the actual ingredients ready to go (Values). You match your ticket to their board to get exactly the right ingredients!

cards.title: Translating French
cards.body: When translating 'Hello world' to 'Bonjour le monde', while writing 'Bonjour', the decoder sends a 'Query' representing the first word. It matches the 'Key' for 'Hello' in the input, and retrieves the 'Value' (the meaning of 'Hello') to help write 'Bonjour'.

cards.title: Not Just Self-Attention!
cards.body: People think cross-attention and self-attention are the exact same thing. But while self-attention only looks at its own sentence, cross-attention connects two completely different sentences together!

cards.title: Encoder vs. Decoder Roles
cards.body: See how information flows between the two sides during translation.

right.text: Creates the new message. Sends the Queries to search the encoder.

left.text: Holds the original message. Provides the Keys and Values for the search.

exercises.prompt: Which component initiates the search in cross-attention by asking 'What should I translate next?'
exercises.explain: In cross-attention, the Queries come from the decoder, which is actively trying to generate the next word in the target language.

exercises.prompt: In cross-attention, the Keys and Values come from the encoder.
exercises.explain: Keys and Values are generated by the encoder from the input text, while the decoder creates the Queries.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing part of the cross-attention flow:
exercises.sentence: Queries are generated by the decoder, while keys and values come from the encoder.
exercises.explain: In cross-attention, the queries come from the decoder (the output generator) to search through the encoder's data.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios does NOT show cross-attention at work?

options.text: An AI model translates Spanish to English by matching English query vectors with Spanish key vectors.
An AI model reads a single sentence and relates each word to other words in the same sentence.
A chatbot looks at your prompt (Keys/Values) while drafting its reply (Queries).

exercises.explain: The second statement describes self-attention, not cross-attention. Cross-attention requires one sequence to query a completely different sequence.

exercises.prompt: Can you explain how cross-attention connects the encoder and the decoder in a translator?

options.text: It only looks at the target language words and ignores the input.
It performs self-attention inside the encoder only.
It uses decoder queries to search through encoder keys and values.
It randomly pairs words together without using queries.

exercises.explain: Cross-attention is the bridge where the decoder's Query looks up information from the encoder's Keys and Values, connecting the two parts of the model.
